subject: concept:neutral-drift-baseline
quote: A meme's mutation rate characterizes the population distribution of its variants, in accordance with the Yule process.
rationale: Abstract states the central empirical finding: variant spread follows the Yule process, the neutral null model.

subject: concept:neutral-drift-baseline
quote: The Yule process is a neutral model where each meme variant has the same probability of copying itself, and possibly mutating again. Notably, most memes follow the Yule process
rationale: Discussion explicitly names the Yule process the neutral model and reports most memes follow it — the operational definition of the neutral-drift baseline.

subject: concept:neutral-drift-baseline
quote: This simple process, previously used to model the number of species per genus, bacterial populations and protein domain family sizes, contains just two simple components: replication and mutation.
rationale: Paper specifies the Yule process as random copying plus mutation with no selection — the no-selection baseline against which selection is detected.

subject: concept:neutral-drift-baseline
quote: Although many variants appear to emerge from neutral drift, there is evidence of some selection, as successful subsequences were found across memes, and individual meme variants were found to match preferences of individuals transmitting them.
rationale: Summary frames selection precisely as the detectable departure from the neutral-drift baseline — the concept's defining role.

subject: concept:replicator-ontology
quote: Richard Dawkins coined the word "meme" to designate ideas or messages that spread and evolve analogously to genes through communication. Determining the extent to which the gene analogy applies to memes has been hampered by the lack of large-scale data
rationale: Intro sets the paper's goal as empirically testing the discrete-copyable-unit (meme-as-replicator) assumption.

subject: concept:replicator-ontology
quote: For textual memes, the string is the information that is being passed, or the 'genotype.' The 'phenotype' is what is expressed by the meme, which can include the meme's message and replication instructions.
rationale: Discussion applies the genotype/phenotype replicator framing to memes — operationalizes the replicator ontology for textual units.

subject: concept:replicator-ontology
quote: When Richard Dawkins introduced the concept of a meme, he suggested that "memes should be regarded as living structures, not just metaphorically but technically". In this study, we apply this concept more rigorously to Facebook memes and find similarities between genes and memes.
rationale: Paper explicitly endorses and rigorously tests the meme-as-replicator premise against empirical data.

subject: concept:meme-ecosystem-competition
quote: Some text sequences can confer a replicative advantage; these sequences are abundant and transfer "laterally" between different memes.
rationale: Abstract documents fitness-conferring sequences moving between competing memes — variants' success is relative to a shared meme population.

subject: concept:meme-ecosystem-competition
quote: exposure to one variant of a meme can make an individual more susceptible to other variants parodying the original variant... This collaborative success of memes has also been observed in a population of image memes.
rationale: Paper shows memes' spread depends on co-circulating memes (susceptibility, collaboration) — fitness is population-relative, not intrinsic.

subject: concept:meme-ecosystem-competition
quote: Subpopulations of the social network can preferentially transmit a specific variant of a meme if the variant matches their beliefs or culture.
rationale: Variants compete for distinct audience niches within the network — relative fitness varies by subpopulation, an ecosystem-competition signature.

_note: Strongly engages neutral-drift-baseline (this IS the empirical Yule-process study) and replicator-ontology. meme-ecosystem-competition is touched via lateral transfer, niche adaptation, and parody-susceptibility, and the paper cites Weng et al. 'Competition among memes in a world with limited attention' [6] and Coscia 'Competition and success in the meme pool' [7]. No confident anchors for superspreader-dynamics (paper's power-law is over variant popularity, not node degree), algorithm-as-selection-engine (2009-2011 copy-paste Facebook, no recommendation feed), epistemic-vigilance, or paradox-of-virality (paper finds variants MATCH subpopulation preferences — closer to the inverse of the paradox).
